draft.introduction: The Dreamtime (Tjukurpa or Jukurrpa) is the foundational concept of Australian Aboriginal spirituality. It describes an eternal, ever-present reality in which ancestral beings shaped the land, established law, and created the patterns that continue to sustain the world.

translation.text: In the Dreaming, the Rainbow Serpent moved across the flat earth, pushing up mountains, carving rivers, creating waterholes. Where she rested, lakes formed. Where she thrashed her tail, valleys opened. She swallowed people and spat them out as new beings, belonging to the land. The land is not property; the land is ancestor. To walk the songlines is to sing the world into continued existence, for the Dreaming is not a past event but the ongoing reality beneath the surface of things.

translator_notes.note: The Dreamtime is not “once upon a time” but an eternal present that underlies all temporal reality. This ontology differs fundamentally from both linear (Abrahamic) and cyclical (Hindu) conceptions of time. The concept of “songlines”—invisible paths across the land that must be ritually sung to sustain creation—implies that human participation is necessary for the world’s continued existence, a striking parallel to the theurgic traditions of late antiquity.
